Decode :  
![][image1]  
The decode stage identifies the type and purpose of an instruction after it is fetched from instruction memory. It examines the instruction bits and determines whether the instruction is an I-type, R-type, S-type, B-type, J-type, or U-type instruction. The decoder then generates the required control signals and selects the source and destination registers needed for execution. This stage ensures that the processor correctly understands the instruction before sending it to the next stage.

Instruction Types Decode :  
![][image2]  
Instruction type decoding is the process of classifying an instruction based on its opcode bits. The decoder checks specific bits of the instruction and matches them with predefined patterns to determine the instruction format. Once the format is identified, the processor knows how to interpret the remaining fields of the instruction, such as registers, immediate values, and operation type. This helps the processor execute different instructions correctly and efficiently.

PROGRAM CODE :  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                    $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                    $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                    $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                    $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
           
   \`BOGUS\_USE($instr);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      //m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule

OUTPUT :   
![][image3]  
VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0qjhOX](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0qjhOX)

LAB Instruction Immediate Decode :  
![][image4]  
Instruction Immediate Decode is the process of extracting the immediate value (imm) from a RISC-V instruction. Different instruction formats store their immediate bits in different positions inside the 32-bit instruction. During the decode stage, the processor identifies the instruction type (I, S, B, U, or J) and rearranges the required bits to create a 32-bit immediate value. The most significant bit (bit 31\) is usually used as the sign bit and is extended to fill the upper bits when needed. This immediate value is then used for arithmetic operations, memory addressing, branch calculations, and jump instructions. In simple terms, immediate decode collects the scattered bits from an instruction and forms a complete value that the processor can use in the next stages of execution.

PROGRAM CODE :  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
         $imm\[31:0\] \= $is\_i\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:20\]} :  
                      $is\_s\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:7\]} :  
                      $is\_b\_instr ? {{20{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[7\], $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:8\], 1'b0} :  
                      $is\_u\_instr ? {$instr\[31:12\], 12'b0} :  
                      $is\_j\_instr ? {{12{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[19:12\], $instr\[20\], $instr\[30:21\], 1'b0} :  
                                    32'b0;  
           
   \`BOGUS\_USE($instr);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      //m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule  
OUTPUT :   
![][image5]

VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0mwh55](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0mwh55)

LAB Instruction Decode :  
![][image6]  
The Instruction Decode stage is an important part of a RISC-V processor. In this stage, the processor examines the fetched instruction and separates it into different fields such as opcode, funct3, funct7, source registers (rs1, rs2), and destination register (rd). These fields help the processor understand what operation needs to be performed and which registers are involved. The decode stage also identifies the instruction type (R-type, I-type, S-type, B-type, U-type, or J-type) and generates control signals required for the next stages of execution.

PROGRAM CODE :  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
         $imm\[31:0\] \= $is\_i\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:20\]} :  
                      $is\_s\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:7\]} :  
                      $is\_b\_instr ? {{20{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[7\], $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:8\], 1'b0} :  
                      $is\_u\_instr ? {$instr\[31:12\], 12'b0} :  
                      $is\_j\_instr ? {{12{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[19:12\], $instr\[20\], $instr\[30:21\], 1'b0} :  
                                    32'b0;  
         $funct7\[6:0\] \= $instr\[31:25\];  
         $funct3\[2:0\] \= $instr\[14:12\];  
         $rs1\[4:0\] \= $instr\[19:15\];  
         $rs2\[4:0\] \= $instr\[24:20\];  
         $rd\[4:0\] \= $instr\[11:7\];  
         $opcode\[6:0\] \= $instr\[6:0\];  
           
   \`BOGUS\_USE($instr);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      //m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule  
OUTPUT :   
![][image7]  
VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0nZhGX](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0nZhGX)

 LAB RISC-V Instruction Field Decode :  
![][image8]  
In this step, the processor extracts individual fields from the 32-bit instruction using their fixed bit positions. Fields such as opcode, funct3, funct7, rs1, rs2, and rd are taken from specific locations in the instruction format. The processor uses conditional logic to determine whether certain fields are valid for a particular instruction type. For example, the rs2 register is only needed for R-type, S-type, and B-type instructions. This decoding process helps the processor correctly interpret the instruction before execution.

PROGRAM CODE :  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
         $imm\[31:0\] \= $is\_i\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:20\]} :  
                      $is\_s\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:7\]} :  
                      $is\_b\_instr ? {{20{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[7\], $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:8\], 1'b0} :  
                      $is\_u\_instr ? {$instr\[31:12\], 12'b0} :  
                      $is\_j\_instr ? {{12{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[19:12\], $instr\[20\], $instr\[30:21\], 1'b0} :  
                                    32'b0;  
         $funct7\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr;  
         ?$funct7\_valid  
            $funct7\[6:0\] \= $instr\[31:25\];  
         $funct3\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         ?$funct3\_valid  
            $funct3\[2:0\] \= $instr\[14:12\];  
         $rs1\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         ?$rs1\_valid  
            $rs1\[4:0\] \= $instr\[19:15\];  
         $rs2\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         ?$rs2\_valid  
            $rs2\[4:0\] \= $instr\[24:20\];  
         $rd\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$rd\_valid  
            $rd\[4:0\] \= $instr\[11:7\];  
         $opcode\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$opcode\_valid  
            $opcode\[6:0\] \= $instr\[6:0\];  
           
   \`BOGUS\_USE($instr);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      //m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule

OUTPUT :   
![][image9]

VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0pghzx](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0pghzx)

LABInstruction Decode and Instruction Identification :  
![][image10]  
After extracting the instruction fields, the processor combines important bits such as opcode, funct3, and funct7 to identify specific instructions. By comparing these values with predefined patterns, it can recognize instructions like BEQ, ADD, and ADDI. For example, a unique combination of opcode and funct3 identifies a BEQ instruction, while another combination identifies an ADD instruction. This allows the processor to determine the exact operation requested by the program and prepare the appropriate control signals for execution. Simulation is then used to verify that the instruction decoding works correctly.

Register File Read :  
![][image11]  
The register file read stage is responsible for fetching the data stored in the source registers required by an instruction. After the instruction is decoded, the source register addresses (rs1 and rs2) are sent to the register file. The register file then reads the corresponding values and makes them available for execution. These values are later used as inputs to the ALU for performing arithmetic or logical operations.

![][image12]  
 PROGRAM CODE :  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
         $imm\[31:0\] \= $is\_i\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:20\]} :  
                      $is\_s\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:7\]} :  
                      $is\_b\_instr ? {{20{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[7\], $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:8\], 1'b0} :  
                      $is\_u\_instr ? {$instr\[31:12\], 12'b0} :  
                      $is\_j\_instr ? {{12{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[19:12\], $instr\[20\], $instr\[30:21\], 1'b0} :  
                                    32'b0;  
         $funct7\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr;  
         ?$funct7\_valid  
            $funct7\[6:0\] \= $instr\[31:25\];  
         $funct3\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         ?$funct3\_valid  
            $funct3\[2:0\] \= $instr\[4:0\];  
         $rs1\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en1 \= $rs1\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index1\[4:0\] \= $rs1\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs1\_valid  
            $rs1\[4:0\] \= $instr\[19:15\];  
         $rs2\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en2 \= $rs2\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index2\[4:0\] \= $rs2\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs2\_valid  
            $rs2\[4:0\] \= $instr\[24:20\];  
         $rd\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$rd\_valid  
            $rd\[4:0\] \= $instr\[11:7\];  
         $opcode\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$opcode\_valid  
            $opcode\[6:0\] \= $instr\[6:0\];  
         $dec\_bits\[10:0\] \= {$funct7\[5\], $funct3, $opcode};  
         $is\_beq \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_1100011;  
         $is\_bne \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_001\_1100011;  
         $is\_blt \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_100\_1100011;  
         $is\_bge \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_101\_1100011;  
         $is\_bltu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_110\_1100011;  
         $is\_bgeu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_111\_1100011;  
         $is\_addi \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_0010011;  
         $is\_add \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'b0\_000\_0110011;  
           
   \`BOGUS\_USE($is\_beq $is\_bne $is\_blt $is\_bge $is\_bltu $is\_bgeu $is\_addi $is\_add);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule

OUTPUT :   
![][image13]  
VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0r0hAn](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0r0hAn)

Register File Read (Part 2\) :  
![][image14]  
In this step, the values read from the source registers are assigned to $src1\_value\[31:0\] and $src2\_value\[31:0\]. These signals carry the actual data stored in the registers and pass it to the next stage of the pipeline. This ensures that the ALU receives the correct operands needed to execute the instruction. The register read operation takes place in pipeline stage @1, preparing the data for processing in the following stage.

PROGRAM CODE :  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
         $imm\[31:0\] \= $is\_i\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:20\]} :  
                      $is\_s\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:7\]} :  
                      $is\_b\_instr ? {{20{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[7\], $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:8\], 1'b0} :  
                      $is\_u\_instr ? {$instr\[31:12\], 12'b0} :  
                      $is\_j\_instr ? {{12{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[19:12\], $instr\[20\], $instr\[30:21\], 1'b0} :  
                                    32'b0;  
         $funct7\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr;  
         ?$funct7\_valid  
            $funct7\[6:0\] \= $instr\[31:25\];  
         $funct3\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         ?$funct3\_valid  
            $funct3\[2:0\] \= $instr\[4:0\];  
         $rs1\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en1 \= $rs1\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index1\[4:0\] \= $rs1\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs1\_valid  
            $rs1\[4:0\] \= $instr\[19:15\];  
         $rs2\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en2 \= $rs2\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index2\[4:0\] \= $rs2\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs2\_valid  
            $rs2\[4:0\] \= $instr\[24:20\];  
         $rd\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$rd\_valid  
            $rd\[4:0\] \= $instr\[11:7\];  
         $opcode\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$opcode\_valid  
            $opcode\[6:0\] \= $instr\[6:0\];  
         $dec\_bits\[10:0\] \= {$funct7\[5\], $funct3, $opcode};  
         $is\_beq \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_1100011;  
         $is\_bne \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_001\_1100011;  
         $is\_blt \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_100\_1100011;  
         $is\_bge \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_101\_1100011;  
         $is\_bltu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_110\_1100011;  
         $is\_bgeu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_111\_1100011;  
         $is\_addi \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_0010011;  
         $is\_add \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'b0\_000\_0110011;  
         $src1\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data1\[31:0\];  
         $src2\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data2\[31:0\];  
           
   \`BOGUS\_USE($is\_beq $is\_bne $is\_blt $is\_bge $is\_bltu $is\_bgeu $is\_addi $is\_add);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule  
OUTPUT :   
![][image15]  
VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0vghW2](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0vghW2)  
ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) :  
![][image16]  
The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) is the part of the processor that performs calculations. After the instruction is decoded and the required register values are read, the ALU receives the source operands and generates the result. For an ADD instruction, it adds the values from two source registers. For an ADDI instruction, it adds a register value and an immediate value from the instruction. The calculated result is stored in $result, which is then sent to the Register File Write stage so it can be saved in the destination register.

PROGRAM CODE :  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
         $imm\[31:0\] \= $is\_i\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:20\]} :  
                      $is\_s\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:7\]} :  
                      $is\_b\_instr ? {{20{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[7\], $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:8\], 1'b0} :  
                      $is\_u\_instr ? {$instr\[31:12\], 12'b0} :  
                      $is\_j\_instr ? {{12{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[19:12\], $instr\[20\], $instr\[30:21\], 1'b0} :  
                                    32'b0;  
         $funct7\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr;  
         ?$funct7\_valid  
            $funct7\[6:0\] \= $instr\[31:25\];  
         $funct3\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         ?$funct3\_valid  
            $funct3\[2:0\] \= $instr\[4:0\];  
         $rs1\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en1 \= $rs1\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index1\[4:0\] \= $rs1\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs1\_valid  
            $rs1\[4:0\] \= $instr\[19:15\];  
         $rs2\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en2 \= $rs2\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index2\[4:0\] \= $rs2\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs2\_valid  
            $rs2\[4:0\] \= $instr\[24:20\];  
         $rd\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$rd\_valid  
            $rd\[4:0\] \= $instr\[11:7\];  
         $opcode\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$opcode\_valid  
            $opcode\[6:0\] \= $instr\[6:0\];  
         $dec\_bits\[10:0\] \= {$funct7\[5\], $funct3, $opcode};  
         $is\_beq \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_1100011;  
         $is\_bne \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_001\_1100011;  
         $is\_blt \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_100\_1100011;  
         $is\_bge \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_101\_1100011;  
         $is\_bltu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_110\_1100011;  
         $is\_bgeu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_111\_1100011;  
         $is\_addi \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_0010011;  
         $is\_add \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'b0\_000\_0110011;  
         $src1\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data1\[31:0\];  
         $src2\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data2\[31:0\];  
         $result\[31:0\] \= $is\_addi ? $src1\_value\[31:0\] \+ $imm\[31:0\] :  
                         $is\_add ? $src1\_value\[31:0\] \+ $src2\_value\[31:0\] :  
                         32'bx;  
           
   \`BOGUS\_USE($is\_beq $is\_bne $is\_blt $is\_bge $is\_bltu $is\_bgeu $is\_addi $is\_add);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule

OUTPUT :   
![][image17]

VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0wjhLP](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0wjhLP)

Register File Write :  
![][image18]  
The Register File Write stage stores the result produced by the ALU into the destination register (rd). When a valid instruction is executed and rd\_valid is asserted, the processor enables register writing and writes $result into the selected register. This allows future instructions to use the newly calculated value. The write operation completes the execution of arithmetic instructions by updating the register file with the final result.

LAB Register File Write :  
![][image19]  
In this lab, the register file write logic is connected so that the ALU result can be written into the destination register. The design must provide the correct write enable signal, destination register index, and write data. The simulation is then checked to ensure that values are being written and read correctly from the registers. Since RISC-V register x0 is always fixed at zero, any attempt to write to register 0 must be ignored. Finally, the completed design is saved outside Makerchip for future use and verification.

PROGRAM CODE:  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
         $imm\[31:0\] \= $is\_i\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:20\]} :  
                      $is\_s\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:7\]} :  
                      $is\_b\_instr ? {{20{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[7\], $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:8\], 1'b0} :  
                      $is\_u\_instr ? {$instr\[31:12\], 12'b0} :  
                      $is\_j\_instr ? {{12{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[19:12\], $instr\[20\], $instr\[30:21\], 1'b0} :  
                                    32'b0;  
         $funct7\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr;  
         ?$funct7\_valid  
            $funct7\[6:0\] \= $instr\[31:25\];  
         $funct3\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         ?$funct3\_valid  
            $funct3\[2:0\] \= $instr\[4:0\];  
         $rs1\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en1 \= $rs1\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index1\[4:0\] \= $rs1\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs1\_valid  
            $rs1\[4:0\] \= $instr\[19:15\];  
         $rs2\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en2 \= $rs2\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index2\[4:0\] \= $rs2\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs2\_valid  
            $rs2\[4:0\] \= $instr\[24:20\];  
         $rd\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$rd\_valid  
            $rd\[4:0\] \= $instr\[11:7\];  
         $opcode\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$opcode\_valid  
            $opcode\[6:0\] \= $instr\[6:0\];  
         $dec\_bits\[10:0\] \= {$funct7\[5\], $funct3, $opcode};  
         $is\_beq \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_1100011;  
         $is\_bne \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_001\_1100011;  
         $is\_blt \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_100\_1100011;  
         $is\_bge \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_101\_1100011;  
         $is\_bltu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_110\_1100011;  
         $is\_bgeu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_111\_1100011;  
         $is\_addi \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_0010011;  
         $is\_add \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'b0\_000\_0110011;  
         $src1\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data1\[31:0\];  
         $src2\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data2\[31:0\];  
         $result\[31:0\] \= $is\_addi ? $src1\_value\[31:0\] \+ $imm\[31:0\] :  
                         $is\_add ? $src1\_value\[31:0\] \+ $src2\_value\[31:0\] :  
                         32'bx;  
         $rf\_wr\_en \= $rd\_valid && ($rd \!= 5'b0);  
         $rf\_wr\_index\[4:0\] \= $rd\[4:0\];  
         $rf\_wr\_data\[31:0\] \= $result\[31:0\];  
   \`BOGUS\_USE($is\_beq $is\_bne $is\_blt $is\_bge $is\_bltu $is\_bgeu $is\_addi $is\_add);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule

OUTPUT :   
![][image20]

VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0xGhJP](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0xGhJP)

Register File – Detailed :  
![][image21]  
This diagram shows how data moves through a simple processor pipeline. First, the Program Counter (PC) provides the address of the instruction to be fetched from Instruction Memory (IMem). The instruction is then decoded to determine which registers are needed. In the next stage, the Register File Read (RF Rd) block reads the required data from the register file. The ALU then performs the required operation, such as addition or subtraction, using the register values. Finally, the result is written back into the register file through the Register File Write (RF Wr) block. This process allows the processor to fetch, decode, execute, and store results efficiently across different pipeline stages.

Arrays – What's Inside? :  
![][image22]  
This diagram explains the internal structure of a simple register array with one read port and one write port. The array contains multiple storage locations, each capable of holding a 32-bit value. When the write enable signal (wr\_en) is active, data (wr\_data) is written into the location specified by the write index (wr\_index). When the read enable signal (rd\_en) is active, the array selects the location specified by the read index (rd\_index) and sends its stored value to the output (rd\_data). Comparators and multiplexers inside the array help identify the correct storage location for reading or writing. In simple terms, the array works like a small memory that stores values and allows the processor to read from or write to specific locations whenever needed.

 LAB Branches :  
![][image23]  
In this lab, branch instructions are added to the RISC-V processor so that the program can make decisions and jump to a different instruction when a condition is true. The processor first checks the branch condition by comparing two register values and generates the signal $taken\_br. This signal becomes 1 when the branch condition is satisfied and 0 otherwise. Different branch instructions use different comparison rules, such as equal to, not equal to, less than, or greater than. After determining whether the branch is taken, the processor calculates the branch target address $br\_tgt\_pc by adding the current Program Counter (PC) value to the immediate offset value. If $taken\_br is true, the PC is updated with the branch target address; otherwise, execution continues with the next sequential instruction

PROGRAM CODE :  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
         $imm\[31:0\] \= $is\_i\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:20\]} :  
                      $is\_s\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:7\]} :  
                      $is\_b\_instr ? {{20{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[7\], $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:8\], 1'b0} :  
                      $is\_u\_instr ? {$instr\[31:12\], 12'b0} :  
                      $is\_j\_instr ? {{12{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[19:12\], $instr\[20\], $instr\[30:21\], 1'b0} :  
                                    32'b0;  
         $funct7\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr;  
         ?$funct7\_valid  
            $funct7\[6:0\] \= $instr\[31:25\];  
         $funct3\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         ?$funct3\_valid  
            $funct3\[2:0\] \= $instr\[4:0\];  
         $rs1\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en1 \= $rs1\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index1\[4:0\] \= $rs1\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs1\_valid  
            $rs1\[4:0\] \= $instr\[19:15\];  
         $rs2\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en2 \= $rs2\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index2\[4:0\] \= $rs2\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs2\_valid  
            $rs2\[4:0\] \= $instr\[24:20\];  
         $rd\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$rd\_valid  
            $rd\[4:0\] \= $instr\[11:7\];  
         $opcode\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$opcode\_valid  
            $opcode\[6:0\] \= $instr\[6:0\];  
         $dec\_bits\[10:0\] \= {$funct7\[5\], $funct3, $opcode};  
         $is\_beq \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_1100011;  
         $is\_bne \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_001\_1100011;  
         $is\_blt \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_100\_1100011;  
         $is\_bge \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_101\_1100011;  
         $is\_bltu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_110\_1100011;  
         $is\_bgeu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_111\_1100011;  
         $is\_addi \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_0010011;  
         $is\_add \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'b0\_000\_0110011;  
         $src1\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data1\[31:0\];  
         $src2\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data2\[31:0\];  
         $taken\_br \= $is\_beq ? ($src1\_value \== $src2\_value) :  
                     $is\_bne ? ($src1\_value \!= $src2\_value) :  
                     $is\_blt ? (($src1\_value \< $src2\_value) ^ ($src1\_value\[31\] \!= $src2\_value\[31\])) :  
                     $is\_bge ? (($src1\_value \>= $src2\_value) ^ ($src1\_value\[31\] \!= $src2\_value\[31\])) :  
                     $is\_bltu ? ($src1\_value \< $src2\_value) :  
                     $is\_bgeu ? ($src1\_value \>= $src2\_value) :  
                     1'b0;  
         $result\[31:0\] \= $is\_addi ? $src1\_value\[31:0\] \+ $imm\[31:0\] :  
                         $is\_add ? $src1\_value\[31:0\] \+ $src2\_value\[31:0\] :  
                         32'bx;  
         $rf\_wr\_en \= $rd\_valid && ($rd \!= 5'b0);  
         $rf\_wr\_index\[4:0\] \= $rd\[4:0\];  
         $rf\_wr\_data\[31:0\] \= $result\[31:0\];  
           
   \`BOGUS\_USE($is\_beq $is\_bne $is\_blt $is\_bge $is\_bltu $is\_bgeu $is\_addi $is\_add);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule  
OUTPUT :   
![][image24]

VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0y8hNK](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0y8hNK)

Branch Target PC :  
![][image25]  
The branch target PC is the address where the processor should jump when a branch is taken. It is calculated by adding the immediate branch offset to the current PC value. The PC multiplexer is then modified so that it selects this target address whenever the previous instruction generated a $taken\_br signal. This allows the processor to correctly change the flow of execution and repeatedly execute instructions inside loops. After implementing this logic, the sample program should correctly calculate the sum of numbers from 1 to 9\.

PROGRAM CODE:  
\\m4\_TLV\_version 1d: tl-x.org  
\\SV  
   // This code can be found in: https://github.com/stevehoover/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop  
     
   m4\_include\_lib(\['https://raw.githubusercontent.com/BalaDhinesh/RISC-V\_MYTH\_Workshop/master/tlv\_lib/risc-v\_shell\_lib.tlv'\])

\\SV  
   m4\_makerchip\_module   // (Expanded in Nav-TLV pane.)  
\\TLV

   // /====================\\  
   // | Sum 1 to 9 Program |  
   // \\====================/  
   //  
   // Program for MYTH Workshop to test RV32I  
   // Add 1,2,3,...,9 (in that order).  
   //  
   // Regs:  
   //  r10 (a0): In: 0, Out: final sum  
   //  r12 (a2): 10  
   //  r13 (a3): 1..10  
   //  r14 (a4): Sum  
   //   
   // External to function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r0, r0)             // Initialize r10 (a0) to 0\.  
   // Function:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r10, r0)            // Initialize sum register a4 with 0x0  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r12, r10, 1010\)         // Store count of 10 in register a2.  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r13, r10, r0)            // Initialize intermediate sum register a3 with 0  
   // Loop:  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r14, r13, r14)           // Incremental addition  
   m4\_asm(ADDI, r13, r13, 1\)            // Increment intermediate register by 1  
   m4\_asm(BLT, r13, r12, 1111111111000\) // If a3 is less than a2, branch to label named \<loop\>  
   m4\_asm(ADD, r10, r14, r0)            // Store final result to register a0 so that it can be read by main program  
     
   // Optional:  
   // m4\_asm(JAL, r7, 00000000000000000000\) // Done. Jump to itself (infinite loop). (Up to 20-bit signed immediate plus implicit 0 bit (unlike JALR) provides byte address; last immediate bit should also be 0\)  
   m4\_define\_hier(\['M4\_IMEM'\], M4\_NUM\_INSTRS)  
     
   |cpu  
      @0  
         $reset \= \*reset;  
         $nxt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ 32'd4;  
         $pc\[31:0\] \= \>\>1$reset ? 32'b0 : \>\>1$taken\_br ? \>\>1$br\_tgt\_pc : \>\>1$nxt\_pc\[31:0\];  
         $imem\_rd\_en \= \!$reset;  
         $imem\_rd\_addr\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT-1:0\] \= $pc\[M4\_IMEM\_INDEX\_CNT+1:2\];  
           
      @1  
         $instr\[31:0\] \= $imem\_rd\_data\[31:0\];  
         $is\_i\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0000x ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b001x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11001;  
         $is\_r\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b01011 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b011x0 ||   
                       $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b10100;  
         $is\_s\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0100x;  
         $is\_b\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11000;  
         $is\_j\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b11011;  
         $is\_u\_instr \= $instr\[6:2\] \==? 5'b0x101;  
         $imm\[31:0\] \= $is\_i\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:20\]} :  
                      $is\_s\_instr ? {{21{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:7\]} :  
                      $is\_b\_instr ? {{20{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[7\], $instr\[30:25\], $instr\[11:8\], 1'b0} :  
                      $is\_u\_instr ? {$instr\[31:12\], 12'b0} :  
                      $is\_j\_instr ? {{12{$instr\[31\]}}, $instr\[19:12\], $instr\[20\], $instr\[30:21\], 1'b0} :  
                                    32'b0;  
         $funct7\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr;  
         ?$funct7\_valid  
            $funct7\[6:0\] \= $instr\[31:25\];  
         $funct3\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         ?$funct3\_valid  
            $funct3\[2:0\] \= $instr\[14:12\];  
         $rs1\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en1 \= $rs1\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index1\[4:0\] \= $rs1\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs1\_valid  
            $rs1\[4:0\] \= $instr\[19:15\];  
         $rs2\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr;  
         $rf\_rd\_en2 \= $rs2\_valid;  
         $rf\_rd\_index2\[4:0\] \= $rs2\[4:0\];  
         ?$rs2\_valid  
            $rs2\[4:0\] \= $instr\[24:20\];  
         $rd\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$rd\_valid  
            $rd\[4:0\] \= $instr\[11:7\];  
         $opcode\_valid \= $is\_r\_instr || $is\_i\_instr || $is\_s\_instr || $is\_b\_instr || $is\_u\_instr || $is\_j\_instr;  
         ?$opcode\_valid  
            $opcode\[6:0\] \= $instr\[6:0\];  
         $dec\_bits\[10:0\] \= {$funct7\[5\], $funct3, $opcode};  
         $is\_beq \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_1100011;  
         $is\_bne \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_001\_1100011;  
         $is\_blt \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_100\_1100011;  
         $is\_bge \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_101\_1100011;  
         $is\_bltu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_110\_1100011;  
         $is\_bgeu \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_111\_1100011;  
         $is\_addi \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'bx\_000\_0010011;  
         $is\_add \= $dec\_bits \==? 11'b0\_000\_0110011;  
         $src1\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data1\[31:0\];  
         $src2\_value\[31:0\] \= $rf\_rd\_data2\[31:0\];  
         $taken\_br \= $is\_beq ? ($src1\_value \== $src2\_value) :  
                     $is\_bne ? ($src1\_value \!= $src2\_value) :  
                     $is\_blt ? (($src1\_value \< $src2\_value) ^ ($src1\_value\[31\] \!= $src2\_value\[31\])) :  
                     $is\_bge ? (($src1\_value \>= $src2\_value) ^ ($src1\_value\[31\] \!= $src2\_value\[31\])) :  
                     $is\_bltu ? ($src1\_value \< $src2\_value) :  
                     $is\_bgeu ? ($src1\_value \>= $src2\_value) :  
                     1'b0;  
         $br\_tgt\_pc\[31:0\] \= $pc\[31:0\] \+ $imm\[31:0\];  
         $result\[31:0\] \= $is\_addi ? $src1\_value\[31:0\] \+ $imm\[31:0\] :  
                         $is\_add ? $src1\_value\[31:0\] \+ $src2\_value\[31:0\] :  
                         32'bx;  
         $rf\_wr\_en \= $rd\_valid && ($rd \!= 5'b0);  
         $rf\_wr\_index\[4:0\] \= $rd\[4:0\];  
         $rf\_wr\_data\[31:0\] \= $result\[31:0\];  
           
   \`BOGUS\_USE($is\_beq $is\_bne $is\_blt $is\_bge $is\_bltu $is\_bgeu $is\_addi $is\_add);

      // Note: Because of the magic we are using for visualisation, if visualisation is enabled below,  
      //       be sure to avoid having unassigned signals (which you might be using for random inputs)  
      //       other than those specifically expected in the labs. You'll get strange errors for these.

     
   // Assert these to end simulation (before Makerchip cycle limit).  
   \*passed \= \*cyc\_cnt \> 40;  
   \*failed \= 1'b0;  
     
   // Macro instantiations for:  
   //  o instruction memory  
   //  o register file  
   //  o data memory  
   //  o CPU visualization  
   |cpu  
      m4+imem(@1)    // Args: (read stage)  
      m4+rf(@1, @1)  // Args: (read stage, write stage) \- if equal, no register bypass is required  
      //m4+dmem(@4)    // Args: (read/write stage)

   m4+cpu\_viz(@4)    // For visualisation, argument should be at least equal to the last stage of CPU logic. @4 would work for all labs.  
\\SV  
   endmodule  
OUTPUT :   
![][image26]

VERIFY LINK :   
[https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/\~04xfJhzq/p-0k5hnZ](https://myth.makerchip.com/v140/ide/~04xfJhzq/p-0k5hnZ)

Testbench :  
![][image27]  
The testbench is used to verify that the processor is working correctly. It monitors the value stored in register x10, which contains the final sum produced by the program. The \*passed signal is set when the value in register x10 becomes equal to the expected result, which is the sum of numbers from 1 to 9\. When this condition is met, Makerchip reports that the simulation has passed. By checking the simulation log, you can confirm that the processor executes the branch instructions correctly and produces the expected output.
